claim_text: The prior state of the art relied heavily on RNN/LSTM/GRU or convolutional sequence transduction models.
Sequential computation limits parallelization in training.
The paper targets long-range dependency modeling and proposes an attention-only alternative.
The Transformer eschews recurrence.
It relies on attention to draw dependencies between input and output.
It improves parallelization and achieves strong translation quality.
The model uses an encoder-decoder structure.
The Transformer uses stacked self-attention layers.
It also uses point-wise fully connected layers in both encoder and decoder.
Experiments use WMT 2014 English-German and English-French data.
Sentences are tokenized with BPE or word-piece vocabularies and batched by approximate sequence length.
Training uses eight P100 GPUs and an Adam schedule with warmup.
No exact total energy-consumption value is reported in the paper.
Prior fine-tuning approaches are restricted by unidirectional language modeling.
Unidirectionality limits architecture choices during pre-training.
Bidirectional context is important for token-level tasks such as question answering.
BERT is a bidirectional Transformer-based language representation model.
Masked language modeling enables deep bidirectional pre-training.
BERT reduces reliance on task-specific architectures and improves results on eleven NLP tasks.
BERT is based on a multi-layer bidirectional Transformer encoder.
Pre-training uses masked language modeling and next-sentence prediction.
Fine-tuning initializes from the pre-trained model and adapts all parameters to downstream tasks.
Experiments fine-tune BERT on eleven NLP tasks.
GLUE evaluation uses task-specific inputs/outputs and a classification layer over the [CLS] vector.
Results are reported on evaluation-server metrics and compared against prior systems.
Standard language models being unidirectional is identified as a major limitation of previous work.
Unidirectionality is suboptimal for sentence-level and token-level tasks.
BERT_LARGE fine-tuning can be unstable on small datasets.
Transfer learning in NLP has produced diverse approaches that are hard to compare.
The paper introduces a text-to-text framework for all text-based language problems.
The unified framing supports systematic comparison of transfer-learning design choices.
The work casts NLP tasks into a unified text-to-text format.
It provides a survey, exploration, and empirical comparison of existing techniques.
It introduces C4 and scales T5 up to achieve strong benchmark results.
Every task is formulated as text input to text output.
The same model/objective/training/decoding process is reused across tasks.
The framework is called T5, the Text-to-Text Transfer Transformer.
The experiments cover diverse tasks including classification, summarization, question answering, and translation.
GLUE/SuperGLUE tasks are combined in the text-to-text setting.
Training uses a consistent maximum-likelihood objective.
The work is primarily a survey/exploration rather than a new-method proposal.
The coordinate-ascent study can miss second-order effects among design choices.
Some automatic benchmark improvements may not correspond to better qualitative behavior.
The target quantity is language-model cross-entropy loss.
Loss is studied as a function of model size, dataset size, and training compute.
The goal is to derive practical scaling relations and compute allocation guidance.
The paper identifies power-law scaling of loss with model size, data size, and compute.
It observes limited effects from some architectural details within the studied range.
It uses the relationships to guide fixed-budget compute allocation.
Models are trained on WebText2 using BPE tokenization.
The principal metric is autoregressive cross-entropy loss.
The main experiments use decoder-only Transformers with controlled scaling parameters.
Model size is varied from 768 to 1.5B non-embedding parameters.
Dataset size is varied from 22M to 23B tokens.
Model shape variables include depth, width, attention heads, and feed-forward dimension.
Generalization of the laws beyond natural language is uncertain.
Predictions such as Bcrit(L) are uncertain outside the explored loss range.
The small-data regime, regularization, and data augmentation were not thoroughly studied.
Fine-tuning typically requires task-specific datasets with many examples.
Humans can often perform new tasks from instructions or a few examples.
The paper studies whether large language models can achieve this kind of in-context learning.
The paper trains and evaluates a 175B-parameter GPT-3 model.
It evaluates zero-shot, one-shot, and few-shot settings across many tasks.
It studies data contamination, scaling behavior, and broader impacts.
The base pre-training approach scales model size, dataset diversity, and training length.
Few-shot learning uses demonstrations in the context without weight updates.
One-shot and zero-shot variants reduce or remove demonstrations while retaining natural-language task descriptions.
The evaluation compares GPT-3 with seven smaller models.
Datasets are grouped into multiple task categories.
Performance is analyzed through scaling curves and task-specific evaluations.
GPT-3 text can repeat, lose coherence, contradict itself, or include non sequiturs.
Some tasks such as ANLI/WIC-style comparison and reading-comprehension tasks remain difficult.
The experiments focus on autoregressive models rather than bidirectional or denoising alternatives.
Large pre-trained models need downstream adaptation.
Manual prompt design is brittle and constrained by context length.
Full model tuning is expensive because it requires task-specific model copies.
Prompt tuning learns soft prompts for frozen language models.
It becomes competitive with full model tuning at large scale.
It supports domain-transfer robustness and prompt ensembling.
The pre-trained model parameters are frozen.
Additional trainable soft-prompt tokens are prepended to the input.
Only prompt parameters are updated during training.
Experiments use T5 checkpoints from Small through XXL.
Baselines include model tuning and multi-task model tuning.
The paper evaluates SuperGLUE and domain-transfer behavior under different prompt-tuning configurations.
No exact total energy-consumption value is reported in the paper.
Full fine-tuning becomes less feasible as pre-trained models grow.
Deploying independent fine-tuned instances of GPT-3-sized models is prohibitively expensive.
The paper seeks a parameter-efficient adaptation method.
LoRA freezes pre-trained weights and trains low-rank matrices.
It reduces trainable parameters and GPU memory requirements.
Its linear design can be merged at inference time with no added latency.
LoRA assumes adaptation updates have low intrinsic rank.
It represents the weight update with low-rank matrices.
The frozen matrix output is augmented as W0x + BAx.
Experiments cover RoBERTa, DeBERTa, GPT-2, and GPT-3.
Tasks include GLUE-style NLU and generation tasks such as WikiSQL/SAMSum.
Baselines include full fine-tuning and other prior adaptation methods.
The mechanism behind fine-tuning and LoRA remains unclear.
Selecting which matrices to adapt currently depends largely on heuristics.
Combining LoRA with other efficient adaptation methods remains a future direction.
Bigger language models do not automatically follow user intent.
Models can generate untruthful, toxic, biased, or unhelpful outputs.
The paper studies fine-tuning with human feedback as an alignment method.
The paper fine-tunes language models with human demonstrations and preferences.
InstructGPT outputs are preferred over GPT-3 outputs in human evaluations.
The paper reports improvements in truthfulness and some toxicity measures.
Start from a pre-trained language model and prompt distribution.
Train a supervised policy on human demonstrations.
Train a reward model from comparisons and optimize the policy with RLHF.
Human labelers evaluate outputs on held-out API prompts.
The paper also uses public NLP datasets and safety-related benchmarks.
It compares 1.3B, 6B, and 175B InstructGPT variants to GPT-3 baselines.
The systems studied are still limited.
The iterative approach does not directly address alignment problems unique to superhuman systems.
Deployment feedback is needed to understand effectiveness and limitations.
Generalization to unseen tasks is the core problem.
Instruction fine-tuning helps models respond to instructions.
The paper explores scaling task count, model size, and chain-of-thought data.
It studies scaling the number of tasks and model size in instruction fine-tuning.
It adds chain-of-thought fine-tuning data.
It reports improvements for Flan-PaLM and Flan-T5 across varied benchmarks and prompting setups.
Flan fine-tunes models on many tasks phrased as instructions.
The fine-tuned models are evaluated on held-out tasks.
Evaluation includes zero-shot, few-shot, and chain-of-thought prompting.
Held-out tasks are excluded from the fine-tuning data.
Benchmarks include MMLU, BBH, TyDiQA, MGSM, and open-ended generation.
Evaluation uses direct prompting, chain-of-thought prompting, exact-match/accuracy, and normalized metrics.
The evaluation does not cover all possible harms and biases.
Automated toxicity measures themselves contain noise and bias.
Toxicity-related findings are limited in scope and mostly English-focused.
The paper targets performance at different inference budgets.
It trains smaller models longer/on more tokens than is typical.
It connects this choice to the trade-off between training cost and inference cost.
LLaMA is a family of 7B to 65B foundation language models.
The models are trained on trillions of tokens from publicly available data.
The paper reports competitive benchmark performance and releases the models for research.
The approach uses large Transformer models.
Training is inspired by Chinchilla-style scaling and uses many tokens.
The data mixture is made from publicly available sources with specified sampling proportions.
The Transformer paper introduces an attention-only architecture without recurrence.
The BERT paper introduces bidirectional Transformer pre-training with masked language modeling.
BERT demonstrates broad downstream gains through fine-tuning, while Transformer focuses on sequence transduction architecture.
The first paper presents the Transformer as an encoder-decoder architecture for sequence transduction.
The second paper uses a bidirectional Transformer encoder as a representation model.
BERT adds pre-training and fine-tuning objectives rather than introducing the original Transformer architecture.
